---
title: "Major, Minor, and the Verdict"
date: "2026-08-10"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
---

# Major, Minor, and the Verdict {.title}

::: notes
Welcome back to peer review and revision, part four of twelve. In part three we finished the report skeleton — identification, specification, exposition. Now, inside that report, we sort every point into major or minor, attach a cure to each major one, and render an honest verdict the editor can act on.
:::

## Sort Every Point: Major or Minor

- Label points under two numbered lists
- Major: could change the conclusion or your belief in it
- Unaddressed threat, wrong standard errors, a suspect filter
- Minor: improves the paper without touching the verdict
- A clearer figure, a missing citation, a confusing sentence

::: notes
Inside the report, sort every point into major or minor, and label them as such — most reports use two numbered lists under those headings. A major point is one whose resolution could change the paper's conclusion or your belief in it: an unaddressed identification threat, a standard-error flavor that inflates the t-statistics, a sample filter that might drive the result, a contribution claim the evidence does not support. A minor point improves the paper without touching the verdict: a clearer figure, a missing citation, a confusing sentence, a table that should report the within R squared. The labeling is a courtesy and a discipline at once. It tells the author where to spend their limited revision energy, and it forces you to decide, for each comment, whether you actually think it could change the result — a sharper question than is this worth mentioning.
:::

## For Each Major Point, State the Cure

- A complaint says "I am not convinced parallel trends holds"
- A contract says exactly what evidence would move you
- "An event-study plot with four pre-period leads would let me judge"
- "Flat leads reassure; a downward pre-trend is close to fatal"
- The contract is the only kind of report worth writing

::: notes
A rule that separates a generous referee from a lazy one: for every major point, say what evidence would change your mind. Do not write, I am not convinced the parallel-trends assumption holds. Write, I am not convinced parallel trends holds; an event-study plot of the denial gap with at least four pre-period leads would let me judge, and flat, insignificant leads would substantially reassure me. The first is a complaint; the second is a contract — it tells the author exactly what to produce and tells them, in advance, that producing it will move you. A referee who states the falsifying or reassuring evidence for every major concern has written a report the author can act on, which is the only kind worth writing.
:::

## Render an Honest, Calibrated Verdict

- Reject, major revision, minor revision, or near-ready
- A clean design stress-tested is a major even with ten fixes
- A design that cannot identify the claim is a reject
- Say "reject as currently identified" plainly — it is kinder
- Vague "promising but needs work" wastes the author's month

::: notes
Separate from the report comes your confidential note to the editor with the verdict. In the camp workshop, this is the line at the top of your report: is this a reject, a major revision, a minor revision, or near-ready? Be honest, and be calibrated. A student paper that ran a clean design on real data, found a result, and stress-tested it is a major or even minor revision even if you found ten things to fix — the bones are sound. A paper whose headline rests on a design that cannot identify the claimed effect is a reject as currently identified, no matter how clean the prose, and saying so plainly is kinder than a vague promising but needs work that wastes the author's next month.
:::

## Key Takeaway {.title}

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Label every point **major** (could change the verdict) or **minor** (does not). For each major point, state the evidence that would change your mind — a *contract*, not a complaint. Then render an honest, calibrated verdict, saying "reject as currently identified" plainly when the design cannot carry the claim. Next: the norm that holds it together — tough on the work, kind to the person.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part four: sort every point into major — could change the verdict — or minor, and label both under numbered lists. For each major point, state the evidence that would change your mind, turning a complaint into a contract the author can act on. Then render an honest, calibrated verdict: a clean, stress-tested design is a major or minor revision even with ten fixes; a design that cannot identify the claim is a reject as currently identified, and saying so plainly is kinder than vague encouragement. In part five we cover the norm that holds the whole enterprise together: tough on the work, kind to the person.
:::
